paper.title: The Great Escape: Discovery of a nearby 1700 km/s star ejected from the Milky Way by Sgr A*

review.summary: Paper contains 2 LaTeX data tables and 2 external software/data resources. Table 1 (tab:hvspar) is the primary object parameter table listing all measured and derived properties of hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1 (astrometry, photometry, spectroscopy, kinematics). Table 2 (tab:sed_fitres) presents SED fitting results from MIST isochrones under photometric-only (Model P) and spectro-photometric (Model SP) models. External resources: rvspecfit software used for radial velocity determination; filter extinction coefficient reference table. Generic Gaia/DPAC acknowledgement URLs excluded per policy. No other structured data tables or data-access URLs found in the paper.

internal_tables.role_in_paper: Primary summary table listing all measured and derived astrometric, photometric, spectroscopic, distance, and kinematic parameters of the hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1. Top section reports Gaia DR2 and survey measurements; bottom section reports this paper's derived quantities (radial velocity, stellar atmosphere parameters, distances, GSR velocities, ejection velocity, predicted proper motions).

source_refs.caption: The measured parameters of the hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1. The top part of the Table refers to the measurements from previous surveys, while the bottom one summarises the measurements presented in the paper. HRV is the heliocentric radial velocity. $\dhel$, $D_{\rm hel,GC}$ are heliocentric distance constraints without and with the Galactocentric origin assumption respectively. $V_{\rm GSR}$, $V_{\rm GSR,GC}$ are the inferred Galactic standard of rest (GSR) velocities of S5-HVS1 determined without and with the Galactocentric origin assumption respectively. $V_{\rm ej,GC}$ is the expected ejection speed from the Galactic Centre. $\mu_{\alpha,\rm pred} \cos \delta$, $\mu_{\delta,\rm pred}$ are the predicted proper motions of S5-HVS1 based on the Galactocentric origin.
source_refs.label: tab:hvspar

columns.name: Parameter
columns.meaning: Name of the measured or derived quantity for S5-HVS1

columns.name: Value
columns.meaning: Measured or derived value for the corresponding parameter, with uncertainty where available

columns.name: unit
columns.meaning: Physical unit of the parameter value

internal_tables.evidence: The measured parameters of the hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1. The top part of the Table refers to the measurements from previous surveys, while the bottom one summarises the measurements presented in the paper. HRV is the heliocentric radial velocity. $\dhel$, $D_{\rm hel,GC}$ are heliocentric distance constraints without and with the Galactocentric origin assumption respectively. $V_{\rm GSR}$, $V_{\rm GSR,GC}$ are the inferred Galactic standard of rest (GSR) velocities of S5-HVS1 determined without and with the Galactocentric origin assumption respectively. $V_{\rm ej,GC}$ is the expected ejection speed from the Galactic Centre. $\mu_{\alpha,\rm pred} \cos \delta$, $\mu_{\delta,\rm pred}$ are the predicted proper motions of S5-HVS1 based on the Galactocentric origin.

internal_tables.role_in_paper: Presents stellar parameters (mass, log10 age, [Fe/H], distance modulus m-M, systematic scatter sigma_sys) derived from fitting MIST isochrones to the S5-HVS1 spectral energy distribution. Two model columns: Photometric-only (Model P) and Spectro-Photometric combined with spectroscopic priors (Model SP).

source_refs.caption: The parameters measured from fitting MIST isochrones to the S5-HVS1 SED (Model P) and by combining SED constraints with spectroscopic constraints (Model SP).
source_refs.label: tab:sed_fitres

columns.name: Value | Photometric
columns.meaning: Best-fit value with upper/lower uncertainties from photometric-only SED fitting (Model P)

columns.name: Value | Spectro-Photometric
columns.meaning: Best-fit value with upper/lower uncertainties from combined SED + spectroscopic prior fitting (Model SP)

columns.name: unit
columns.meaning: Physical unit of the parameter value (M_sun for mass, dex for log age and [Fe/H], mag for distance modulus and systematic scatter)

internal_tables.evidence: The parameters measured from fitting MIST isochrones to the S5-HVS1 SED (Model P) and by combining SED constraints with spectroscopic constraints (Model SP).

external_resources.description: RVSpecFit software used by the S5 survey for spectral modelling to determine radial velocities and stellar atmospheric parameters from AAT/AAOmega spectra. Applied to S5-HVS1 for simultaneous blue+red arm fitting to derive Teff, log g, and [Fe/H].

source_refs.context:
The data processing of the \SSSSS data includes standard data reduction steps by the AAT pipeline, followed by spectral modelling by the \code{rvspecfit}\footnote{\url{http://github.com/segasai/rvspecfit}} software in order to determine the radial velocities and stellar atmospheric parameters. 

\subsection{HVS star search}

external_resources.evidence: The data processing of the \SSSSS data includes standard data reduction steps by the AAT pipeline, followed by spectral modelling by the \code{rvspecfit}\footnote{\url{http://github.com/segasai/rvspecfit}} software in order to determine the radial velocities and stellar atmospheric parameters.

external_resources.description: Reference table of extinction coefficients k_i for photometric filters used in the MIST isochrone SED fitting model (Equation 1). The coefficients are applied to the E(B-V) reddening value in each band i.

source_refs.context: where $\sigma_i$ is the uncertainty on the magnitude measurement in band $i$,  $\sigma_{\mathrm{sys}}$ is an additional (systematic) scatter around the model, $D_{\mathrm{hel}}$ is the heliocentric distance to the star, and $k_i$ is the extinction coefficient\footnote{Taken from \url{http://www.mso.anu.edu.au/~brad/filters.html}} in the filter $i$.

external_resources.evidence: where $\sigma_i$ is the uncertainty on the magnitude measurement in band $i$,  $\sigma_{\mathrm{sys}}$ is an additional (systematic) scatter around the model, $D_{\mathrm{hel}}$ is the heliocentric distance to the star, and $k_i$ is the extinction coefficient\footnote{Taken from \url{http://www.mso.anu.edu.au/~brad/filters.html}} in the filter $i$.
